metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the `sso-gate` Terraform module exists in `infra/terraform/` (or confirm its expected path after the adoption story lands) before authoring the runbook section that documents it; if it doesn't exist yet, scope the runbook to omit or stub that section with an explicit 'TODO: add after sso-gate module is merged' note.
findings.finding: The plan's runbook requirement states it should cover 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module,' but no `sso-gate` module exists anywhere in the repository. A full-repo grep for 'sso-gate' returns zero matches.
findings.location: What section: 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `sso-gate` module reference: either confirm it is created by the sibling terraform-adoption story (and note the dependency), or replace the reference with the actual pattern/resource names that will exist after adoption (e.g., the CloudFront + Lambda authorizer pattern used by auth_host).
findings.finding: The plan's 'What' section directs the runbook author to document 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module', but no `sso-gate` Terraform module exists anywhere in infra/terraform/** (or elsewhere in the repo). The runbook would be referencing a non-existent module.
findings.location: What section: 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module'
findings.suggested_fix: If the sso-gate pattern is the auth_host Lambda+CloudFront pattern from snap, name it concretely in the runbook outline (e.g., 'reuse the `snap-demo-auth-host` CloudFront+Lambda authorizer pattern') rather than implying a standalone Terraform module called `sso-gate`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the `sso-gate` Terraform module exists (or will land alongside this ticket) before committing to documenting its interface in AC4's runbook; if it doesn't exist yet, gate this section on the terraform adoption story.
findings.finding: The What section references 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module' as a required runbook section, but no `sso-gate` module exists anywhere in `infra/terraform/` per the directory listing. This is an unverified capability assertion — the module may not yet exist at the time this documentation ticket is executed.
findings.location: What section — 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module'; Acceptance Criteria AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit dependency note: 'sso-gate module runbook section requires the terraform adoption story to land first' and either defer that subsection to a follow-on ticket or confirm the module's inputs/outputs are available for documentation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a conditional guard to the AC: the iam.tf comment must only be updated AFTER the `resource "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider"` block is confirmed present in `infra/terraform/iam.tf` (i.e., after the adoption Terraform story lands); verify with a grep before merging this doc change.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'rebar now OWNS the GitHub OIDC provider' and instructs the docs to reflect this, but no `resource "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider"` HCL block exists anywhere in `infra/terraform/**`. The iam.tf file only contains a `data` reference via a hardcoded ARN string. The plan's What/AC items say to update the comment away from 'already exists / assumes it exists,' but the underlying ownership claim — that rebar creates/manages the provider in Terraform — is not backed by any extant resource block.
findings.location: What section ('rebar now OWNS the GitHub OIDC provider') and AC: '`infra/terraform/iam.tf`'s OIDC comment no longer claims the provider is externally owned'
findings.suggested_fix: Make the AC explicitly conditional: 'After the terraform adoption story lands and `resource "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider"` is confirmed in iam.tf, update the comment.' Alternatively, sequence this plan to depend on the adoption story ticket being closed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete proving command (e.g., `grep -i 'owns\|created by rebar' docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md`, `grep -v 'assumes it exists' infra/terraform/iam.tf`, `ls docs/adr/*oidc* infra/runbooks/*sso*`) for each AC so completion is verifiable without reading every file.
findings.finding: No proving commands are specified for any acceptance criterion. For AC1 and AC4 (doc files), AC2 (comment removal), and AC3 (ADR content), the plan provides no concrete verification command (e.g., grep, find, or content assertion) that would produce evidence of completion.
findings.suggested_fix:
Append a 'How to verify' block to each AC:
- AC1: `grep -iE 'rebar.*(own|creat)' docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md`
- AC2: `grep -c 'assumes it exists' infra/terraform/iam.tf` → expect 0
- AC3: `ls docs/adr/ | grep -i 'snap.*rebar\|rebar.*snap\|re.hom'`
- AC4: each required topic (architecture, secrets, rotation, incident-revoke) has a corresponding section heading: `grep -iE '^#+.*(architect|secret|rotat|revok)' infra/runbooks/sso-operations.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the `infra/terraform/iam.tf` AC to verify BOTH removal of the stale 'already exists / assumes it exists' text AND the presence of a replacement comment asserting rebar ownership.
findings.finding: The `infra/terraform/iam.tf` AC only verifies REMOVAL of the stale claim ('no longer claims the provider is externally owned') but does not verify what REPLACEMENT text is required, even though the What section implies the comment should now assert rebar ownership.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, bullet 2: '`infra/terraform/iam.tf`'s OIDC comment no longer claims the provider is externally owned.'
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC to: '`infra/terraform/iam.tf`'s OIDC comment no longer claims the provider is externally owned and instead states that rebar creates and owns the provider.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC 4 to also require the runbook covers how to protect a new rebar subdomain using the sso-gate module.
findings.finding: The runbook Acceptance Criterion omits the 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module' content that is explicitly described in the What section, leaving that described deliverable partially uncovered.
findings.location: What section, SSO runbook bullet vs. AC bullet 4
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC 4 to: 'An `infra/runbooks/` SSO runbook covers architecture, secrets, rotation, incident revoke, and how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit sequencing guard to AC2: specify that the iam.tf comment should only be updated to assert rebar ownership **after** (or in the same PR as) the `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` resource block is added to the HCL by the terraform adoption story.
findings.finding: The plan fixes the iam.tf comment from 'already exists in the account' to assert rebar ownership, but iam.tf currently contains no `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` resource block (confirmed by codebase search). If this doc-only plan lands before the terraform adoption story, the updated comment will assert rebar manages the provider when the HCL still has no such resource — replacing one stale claim with a false one. The plan acknowledges the dependency ('Depends on the terraform adoption story landing or landing alongside') but provides no guard ensuring the comment fix is applied only after or atomically with the HCL that actually creates the resource.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section and AC2, change the iam.tf comment fix to be conditional: e.g., 'Update the iam.tf comment to reflect rebar ownership **only as part of, or after, the terraform adoption story that adds the `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` resource block**; until then, the comment should note the provider is managed externally pending adoption.' Alternatively, combine this AC into the terraform adoption story's PR.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC for the retag: either 'docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md and/or the SSO runbook note the Project tag change from `project=snap-demo` to `Project=rebar`' (if documentation-only) or explicitly call it out-of-scope in the Scope section.
findings.finding: The 'What' section includes a retag work item (`project=snap-demo` → `Project=rebar`) but no corresponding acceptance criterion exists, leaving this deliverable unmeasured and potentially dropped.
findings.location: What section (last bullet) vs. Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] The SSO runbook (or gerrit-aws-setup.md) documents the tag change from `project=snap-demo` to `Project=rebar` and its rationale.
OR add to Scope: 'The retag itself is out of scope for this ticket; it is tracked in <infra ticket X>.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default posture explicitly in the Scope section, e.g. 'This documentation work may proceed and merge in parallel with the terraform adoption story; final merge should be gated on the terraform story landing.'
findings.finding: The dependency statement 'Depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside)' is a defaultable ambiguity: an executor cannot determine whether to proceed independently or wait for a prerequisite, as no default posture is stated.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside).' with: 'This documentation work may be authored and reviewed in parallel with the terraform adoption story (link: <ticket>); the PR should not be merged until the terraform adoption story lands so references are accurate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion verifying the retag from `project=snap-demo` to `Project=rebar` is noted/applied (e.g., in the updated terraform or doc).
findings.finding: The retag note (`project=snap-demo` → `Project=rebar`) is described in the What section but has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion to verify it was done.
findings.location: What section (last bullet) vs. Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '`infra/terraform/` resource tags (or their documentation) reflect `Project=rebar` rather than `project=snap-demo`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm snap's `auth/RUNBOOK.md` is accessible (e.g., note where it is archived or paste its key sections into the plan/ADR) so the executor can adapt it for AC4.
findings.finding: The runbook (AC4) is described as 'adapted from snap's auth/RUNBOOK.md', but snap's repo is explicitly called a 'dead repo' in the Why section. The plan provides no confirmation that this source document is archived, mirrored, or otherwise accessible to the executor — if it is gone, the runbook content foundation is unavailable.
findings.location: What section — 'Add an SSO operations runbook under infra/runbooks/ adapted from snap's auth/RUNBOOK.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the What section indicating where the source RUNBOOK.md is preserved (e.g., a specific commit hash in snap's archived repo, or a snapshot attached to this ticket). If snap's repo is fully gone, acknowledge the runbook must be reconstructed from the terraform adoption story's artifacts.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a call-out in the plan (or a dedicated artifact such as `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate/README.md`) to document the sso-gate module's input/output contract, so consumers have a stable interface reference independent of the ops runbook.
findings.finding: The `sso-gate` module is introduced as a reusable pattern for protecting rebar subdomains, but no module-level documentation (e.g., a Terraform module README with input/output contracts) is called for — only a runbook how-to. Teams adopting the module need a stable interface contract, not just operational prose.
findings.location: What section (runbook bullet: 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module') / Acceptance Criteria (SSO runbook AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet under What: 'Ensure `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate/README.md` (or equivalent) documents the module's required/optional variables and outputs.' Add a corresponding AC: 'sso-gate module interface is documented at the module level.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign the retag fact to a specific artifact (e.g., the ADR's 'tradeoffs' section or the runbook's 'architecture' section) and add a corresponding AC line confirming it is recorded there.
findings.finding: The retag change (`project=snap-demo` → `Project=rebar`) is listed in the What section but is not assigned to any specific documentation artifact (ADR, runbook, or updated doc), leaving it potentially unrecorded in rebar's written record.
findings.location: What section, last bullet: 'Note the retag (project=snap-demo → Project=rebar)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the ADR outline: a note that AWS resource tags were migrated from `project=snap-demo` to `Project=rebar`, including the rationale (ownership transfer, billing attribution). Add an AC: 'ADR or runbook records the tag migration and its operational significance.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and record the parent ticket ID in the plan text (or ticket metadata), then verify that all four leaf ACs — updating docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md, fixing the iam.tf OIDC comment, adding the re-homing ADR, and adding the SSO runbook — are each covered by or consistent-narrowings of the parent's stated What/Scope/ACs.
findings.finding: The parent ticket cannot be located: the plan text does not embed a parent_id, and no ticket in the system could be found matching the 'terraform adoption story' (snap→rebar OIDC provider + auth_host SSO adoption) that this leaf claims to depend on. Without the parent's What/Scope/ACs, the G7 containment check — verifying the leaf's declared scope is a consistent SUBSET of the parent's contract — cannot be completed.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Documentation only. Depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `parent_id:` reference to the ticket metadata pointing at the terraform adoption story, then re-run G7 against the located parent. If the parent is not yet created, create it first (per the CONFLICT RULE: the parent is the authoritative contract; the leaf may not exist independently of it for G7 purposes).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a requirement to AC4 (and the runbook spec in 'What') that the runbook must explicitly declare the access posture of the auth_host SSO stack — specifically: what principals/users may authenticate, what the gate enforces (e.g. Google OAuth domain restriction), and whether any endpoint is intentionally public — so operators have a stated trust model alongside the rotation/revoke procedures.
findings.finding: The SSO operations runbook AC specifies coverage of 'architecture, secrets, rotation, and incident revoke' for a public-internet-facing, domain-wide SSO stack (CloudFront + API GW + Lambda), but never requires the runbook to declare the access posture of that stack — i.e., who is permitted to reach the SSO endpoint, what authentication/authorization gate it enforces, and whether any path is intentionally public. For a boundary-crossing system this undeclared access posture is the T5c failure mode: the runbook will tell operators how to rotate secrets and pull the revoke lever but not what the system is actually protecting or what its trust model is.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'An `infra/runbooks/` SSO runbook covers architecture, secrets, rotation, and incident revoke.'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC4 to read: 'An `infra/runbooks/` SSO runbook covers architecture (including declared access posture: who may authenticate, what the gate enforces, and any intentionally public paths), secrets, rotation, and incident revoke.' Add a matching bullet to the 'What' section runbook spec.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
